summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that a low-privileged authenticated user can create a workflow with an ExecuteCommand node that executes arbitrary shell commands on the host system, demonstrating complete code execution capabilities.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the ExecuteCommand node to execute attacker-controlled shell commands with Node.js process privileges, creating proof files and reading sensitive system files.
actual_observed_result: The reproduction successfully created an n8n instance, created a workflow with an ExecuteCommand node containing malicious shell commands, executed the workflow, and observed the RCE proof marker 'RCE_SUCCESS:node' in the container filesystem, confirming arbitrary command execution on the host system.
verification_method: The run script executes the exploit trigger and checks the container filesystem for proof files (/tmp/rce_marker.txt, /tmp/rce_proof.txt, /tmp/rce_result.txt) created by the executed commands. This distinguishes successful command execution from normal workflow operation or errors.
evidence_produced_by_run: Container filesystem contains /tmp/rce_marker.txt with 'RCE_SUCCESS:node' proof marker
Container filesystem contains /tmp/rce_proof.txt with output from 'id' command showing user context
Container filesystem contains /tmp/rce_result.txt with first line of /etc/passwd, confirming sensitive file read
The workflow was successfully created by an authenticated user with basic authentication credentials
No high-privilege access or special deployment conditions were required for the exploit
Commands executed with full Node.js process privileges without sandboxing or restrictions

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the n8n repository from GitHub on the host, checks out the vulnerable version tag v1.123.21, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
